app.name: QuickBooks Online
app.bestFor: Default choice for small businesses that work with a CPA, with mature AI transaction categorization and the widest ecosystem.
app.rankBasis: The established default across the sampled bookkeeping roundups, backed by 3,700+ QuickBooks Online reviews on G2 and the largest U.S. accountant and app ecosystem in this set.
app.pricingPaid: Simple Start $38/mo — 1 user, automated bookkeeping, invoicing, bank feeds and expense tracking
features: Invoicing, bill pay and expense tracking
pros: widest accountant familiarity and third-party integration ecosystem
broad small-business accounting feature set in one product
reviewers frequently praise accessibility and everyday ease of use
cons: frequent price increases and plan limits are recurring community complaints
support quality and resolution times receive mixed reviews
interface changes and occasional sluggishness frustrate long-time users

app.name: Xero
app.bestFor: Growing teams that want unlimited users, a clean interface, and strong third-party integrations.
app.rankBasis: A consistent top-two roundup choice with a 4.4/5 G2 seller rating from roughly 1,674 verified reviews; G2 also scores its ease of use at 92% and ease of setup at 90%.
app.pricingPaid: Early $25/mo — bank reconciliation, invoicing, bill entry and standard ACH bill payments, with no per-user license fee
pros: unlimited users make collaboration economical
reviewers consistently praise fast, intuitive bank reconciliation
large integration marketplace and clean cloud workflow
cons: some reviewers find the interface clunky and onboarding guidance thin
advanced inventory, payroll and some expense workflows require add-ons or partners
users report occasional bugs and unresolved support issues

app.name: Puzzle
app.bestFor: AI-native accounting for startups on modern fintech stacks (Stripe, Mercury, Ramp), automating up to 98% of transactions.
app.rankBasis: Included across the sampled AI-accounting roundups and praised in founder/bookkeeper community discussion for real-time visibility, but major review-platform volume remains limited versus incumbents.
app.pricingFree: Accounting Basics $0 — 1 user, cash and accrual books, automatic bank reconciliation and a tax package; aimed at pre-revenue companies under $5k monthly expenses
app.pricingPaid: Core $60/mo billed yearly — multi-user access, cash/burn/runway insights, no integration limits and multi-entity support via Joiin
features: Real-time cash, burn and runway insights
pros: modern interface makes startup financials easier for non-accountants to understand
strong integrations with Mercury, Stripe, Ramp, Brex and payroll tools
real-time books and AI review can shorten month-end close
cons: independent verified-review volume is still thin
non-U.S. bank accounts and true multi-currency workflows are not supported
Puzzle does not provide bookkeeping services or file taxes itself

app.name: Digits
app.bestFor: Built-from-scratch AI bookkeeping with 24/7 auto-categorization (reported ~96.5% accuracy) and real-time dashboards.
app.rankBasis: A recurring AI-bookkeeping roundup pick with a 4.8/5 G2 rating, though from only 5 reviews; community threads show interest but also caution about trusting a still-maturing AI ledger.
app.pricingPaid: Essentials $65/mo per business — live financials, invoicing and bill pay, 24/7 AI bookkeeping and reconciliation, integrations, API/MCP and mobile access
pros: reviewers praise rapid setup and a polished AI-native interface
real-time financials reduce routine categorization work
no per-seat fees and an extensible API/MCP
cons: the 4.8 G2 score is based on only five reviews
feature breadth is still narrower than mature accounting suites
U.S.-only availability and no tax filing limit its fit

app.name: Zeni
app.bestFor: Full-service AI finance for startups, pairing automated bookkeeping with CFO-level insight (cash runway, burn rate) and human support.
app.rankBasis: The strongest reviewed full-service entrant here: it appears in multiple AI-accounting roundups and holds about 4.7/5 on G2 from 73 reviews, with praise centered on dashboards and real-time visibility.
app.pricingPaid: Starter $549/mo plus $2,000 setup — AI bookkeeping for pre-revenue companies with a dedicated bookkeeping manager and analyst
features: AI bookkeeping with human finance-team review
Real-time cash, burn and runway dashboards
pros: combines automation with a dedicated human finance team
reviewers praise centralized reporting and real-time startup metrics
broad service coverage can replace several finance vendors
cons: high monthly price and mandatory setup fee
primarily designed for U.S.-based startups rather than general SMBs
pricing rises with bookkeeping complexity, expenses and revenue

app.name: Ramp
app.bestFor: Finance ops platform (cards, expenses, bill pay) whose Accounting Agent auto-codes the GL and pushes in-policy spend to your ERP.
app.rankBasis: The best-supported finance-ops choice by review volume: Ramp Financial has a 4.8/5 G2 seller rating from roughly 2,452 verified reviews, with repeated praise for expense usability and automation.
app.pricingFree: Free $0/user — corporate cards, expense management, bill pay, approval workflows, accounting integrations and AI reporting
app.pricingPaid: Plus $15/user/mo — adds AI approval recommendations, advanced controls, payment-release approvals and expanded workflows
features: AI receipt capture and policy enforcement
pros: large review base consistently praises its intuitive expense workflow
substantial card, bill-pay and accounting functionality is free
automation reduces receipt chasing and manual coding
cons: it complements rather than replaces a general ledger
advanced controls become per-user costs on Plus
legacy-system integrations and broader procurement rollouts can be complex

app.name: Botkeeper
app.bestFor: Accounting-firm platform that pairs ML transaction handling with CPA review of the outputs.
app.rankBasis: A specialist accounting-firm platform with 30 G2 reviews and 71 verified Capterra reviews; reviewers value tedious-work automation, while glitches and categorization quality remain recurring caveats.
app.pricingPaid: Infinite $149/client entity/mo month-to-month ($134/mo billed annually for 1–4 licenses) — transaction manager, transaction insights and client/user management
features: Autonomous month-end review and anomaly detection
pros: automates repetitive transaction work for accounting firms
reviewers praise current books, management reports and time savings
volume pricing supports multi-client firm workflows
cons: output quality depends on the accuracy of the existing general ledger
unmatched transactions can land in miscellaneous or unassigned accounts
reviewers report occasional glitches and inconsistent support resolution

skill.name: ERPNext
skill.bestFor: Most-adopted open-source accounting/ERP (~37k★) — a full double-entry accounting module (GL, invoicing, tax, reports) positioned as the OSS QuickBooks/SAP alternative for SMBs.
skill.rankBasis: The clear adoption leader in this track at 37,223 GitHub stars, with repeated inclusion in open-source accounting roundups and active ERP/self-hosting community discussion.
pros: unusually complete business suite around the accounting core
highly customizable through Frappe, APIs and custom apps
large open-source community and implementation ecosystem
cons: implementation, upgrades and process design require real ERP expertise
community users report uneven or outdated documentation
self-hosting adds operational and database administration work

skill.name: Firefly III
skill.bestFor: The recognized self-hosted double-entry finance manager (~24k★); the go-to open-source app for tracking accounts, budgets and transactions.
skill.rankBasis: The dominant self-hosted personal-finance ledger at 24,120 GitHub stars, widely discussed in r/selfhosted comparisons and repeatedly recommended for feature depth and privacy.
pros: rich feature set with full control of financial data
powerful rules, reports and automation for detailed tracking
active project with mobile and importer ecosystem
cons: built for personal finance rather than business invoicing or payroll
community users find setup and the separate importer unintuitive
investment tracking is limited and often requires manual workarounds

skill.name: Akaunting
skill.bestFor: Popular self-hosted web accounting for small businesses and freelancers (~10k★): invoicing, expense tracking, banking and tax management.
skill.rankBasis: A leading web-based OSS accounting option with 9,975 GitHub stars and regular roundup coverage, though self-hosting communities frequently flag its paid-app model.
pros: accessible browser-based interface for small businesses
modular ecosystem covers many country and workflow needs
self-hosting keeps core financial data under user control
cons: critical capabilities such as double-entry and bank feeds may require paid apps
add-on costs accumulate quickly despite the free core
users report upgrade, support and self-hosted app compatibility problems

skill.name: Invoice Ninja
skill.bestFor: Widely used self-hosted invoicing, billing and expense platform (~9.9k★) for freelancers and small businesses.
skill.rankBasis: A mature invoicing specialist with 9,906 GitHub stars, broad self-hosting recognition and a long-running community around its hosted and open-source editions.
pros: polished invoicing workflow with many payment gateways
hosted and self-hosted deployment choices
strong automation for freelancers and service businesses
cons: not a full double-entry general ledger
self-hosted upgrades, mail and payment configuration require maintenance
community feedback notes occasional UI and mobile workflow friction

skill.name: Ledger
skill.bestFor: The original plain-text, command-line double-entry accounting tool (~6k★); the foundation of the plaintext-accounting movement.
skill.rankBasis: The original plaintext-accounting standard with 5,994 GitHub stars and enduring mindshare in the plaintextaccounting community, where later tools still inherit its journal format.
features: Flexible balance, register and income reports
pros: fast, portable data that works naturally with version control
powerful reporting and automation for technical users
stable format with a mature surrounding ecosystem
cons: steep command-line and accounting-syntax learning curve
no built-in GUI, bank sync or collaborative workflow
imports and data cleanup generally require scripts or manual work

skill.name: Beancount
skill.bestFor: Plain-text double-entry accounting language and toolset (~5.8k★, with the Fava web UI); favored by developers who script and automate their books.
skill.rankBasis: A major developer-oriented plaintext ledger with 5,835 GitHub stars and a dedicated community that commonly pairs it with Fava for browser-based analysis.
features: Fava web reports and visualization ecosystem
pros: strict data model catches errors early
highly programmable for custom imports and analysis
plain-text portability plus a strong Fava web interface
cons: community users describe the documentation as difficult to parse
budgeting and forecasting are not built in
useful automation often requires Python and custom import rules

skill.name: hledger
skill.bestFor: Robust, actively maintained plain-text accounting tool (~4.6k★) — a Ledger-compatible reimplementation with strong CLI, web and reporting.
skill.rankBasis: The most active Ledger-compatible alternative at 4,595 GitHub stars, with recurring plaintextaccounting recommendations for its maintained CLI, terminal UI and web interface.
pros: actively maintained with several interfaces over one journal
portable data works well with version control
strong reporting and repeatable CSV import rules
cons: still aimed primarily at technically confident users
no managed bank feeds or turnkey collaboration
business workflows such as invoicing and payroll require other tools

skill.name: GnuCash
skill.bestFor: Mature desktop double-entry accounting (~4.3k★) for personal and small-business use, with invoicing, investments and reports.
skill.rankBasis: The mature desktop benchmark with 4,293 GitHub stars, decades of usage and frequent open-source accounting recommendations for its documentation and complete double-entry model.
pros: mature, comprehensive accounting engine
strong documentation and long-established user community
handles both small-business books and investments
cons: dated desktop interface is a common community complaint
local-file architecture is awkward for team collaboration
bank imports and account setup require more manual work than cloud tools

skill.name: Bigcapital
skill.bestFor: Open-source accounting and inventory platform (~3.8k★) positioned as a QuickBooks/Xero/Wave alternative with intelligent financial reporting.
skill.rankBasis: A newer modern-web contender with 3,794 GitHub stars and open-source accounting roundup coverage, but substantially less independent community evidence than the projects above it.
pros: modern web interface compared with older OSS accounting tools
combines accounting and inventory in one system
API-first design supports custom integrations
cons: smaller community and thinner independent review history
self-hosting the multi-service stack requires technical effort
documentation and ecosystem are less mature than ERPNext or GnuCash

notes: App track has strong multi-source consensus. There is no dedicated Agent Skill (SKILL.md) ecosystem for bookkeeping, so the skill track is filled with notable open-source accounting repos (kind:repo) an agent can run or integrate against, ranked strictly by real adoption/reputation (GitHub stars) rather than form factor: ERPNext (~37k★, full accounting/ERP, the OSS QuickBooks analog) leads, followed by Firefly III (~24k★, the recognized self-hosted bookkeeping manager) and the popular business/plaintext accounting tools. maybe-finance (~54k★) was excluded as archived/discontinued; actualbudget was excluded as budgeting rather than bookkeeping; apache/fineract (core banking) is out of scope. MCP servers and vendor API connectors are intentionally excluded from this track.
